title: The Channel-Count Confound: A Continental Audit of Multi-Horizon Urban Growth Prediction
status: Accepted. Sole author. PDF coming soon at SpatialDI.
abstract: A continental audit of CNN and ConvLSTM comparisons on multi-horizon urban-growth prediction across 305M CONUS pixels. Under sealed 2020 temporal holdouts, zero-shot transfer to Lagos, and MC Dropout calibration over 8.69M validation pixels, the apparent ConvLSTM advantage at longer horizons collapses. It was a channel-count artefact, not a representational benefit. Per-tile Wilcoxon and bootstrap confirmation; calibration r = 0.983.

title: Channel Count, Not Horizon Length, Drives Architectural Divergence in Multi-Horizon Urban Growth Prediction
status: Accepted. Sole author. 6-page short paper companion to the SpatialDI main paper. PDF on Zenodo.
abstract: A short-form companion to the SpatialDI main paper. Distils the channel-count finding into a six-page argument: when forecast horizon and input-channel count move together, what looks like a horizon-length effect in CNN vs ConvLSTM comparisons is largely an input-design artefact. Channel-matched controls collapse the apparent gap.

title: Cross-Region Source-Invariance in Earth Observation
status: In preparation. Sole author. Follow-on to the channel-count audit.
abstract: A source-by-target study of what actually decides whether a geospatial model transfers between regions. One model is trained per region and every model is evaluated on every region, spanning twenty world regions, eight prediction tasks, and several input representations. Two results hold across the matrix. First, transfer is governed by the data, not the model: the test region and the input representation explain almost all of the cross-region score variance while the training region explains almost none, and a parameter-free baseline that extrapolates each region's recent past matches or beats the trained models on temporal tasks. Second, when the input is held fixed and swapped, retention falls along a spectrum set by how region-invariant the input-to-label mapping is, with harmonized products at the top, raw single-sensor inputs at the bottom, and SAR holding up better than expected. Controls cover deflation baselines, input-swap provenance, change-rate confounds, covariates, and pretrained foundation models. Built on GHSL, Landsat, Sentinel-1/2, MODIS NDVI and LST, ESA WorldCover, Copernicus DEM, OpenStreetMap, and the PANGAEA benchmark.

title: UrbanFinance: Forecasting the Latent Land-Demand Residual at 100 m across Divergent Indian Urban Typologies
abstract: Rather than predicting realized built-up area or price, both well covered, this work predicts the signed gap between economically expressed demand and the supply that Earth observation actually records: the cells where the demand signal runs hot but conversion never happened, held back by zoning, litigation, geography, or vacancy. That residual is what demand for land over time really means. Five deliberately divergent metros, Noida, Mumbai, Chandigarh, Bengaluru, and Hyderabad, stress-test cross-typology generalization on a free, open 100 m channel stack that fuses GHSL supply layers, population and night-lights demand signals, terrain and water constraints, and NHB RESIDEX price indices. The proposed DSRF model beats SimVP, TAU, and PredRNN.V2 on Figure of Merit at p<0.05 over ten seeds, yields calibrated 95% prediction intervals, and under leave-one-city-out transfers to an unseen city better than SOTA models trained on all five. An undevelopable-land measure built from terrain and water recovers a correct-signed supply economics: constrained cities convert less land (corr -0.77) and carry more suppressed demand (corr +0.79).

title: Understanding Academic Collaboration Networks through Small World Theory
abstract: An empirical study of academic collaboration structure across 12 domains and 180K+ co-authorship edges, applying small-world statistics to test whether high-impact researchers concentrate within short network neighborhoods. Includes reproducible pipelines for citation-graph construction and author-impact inference.
